introduction: The Chemistry Virtual Laboratory provides an interactive platform for understanding and performing analytical techniques used in the identification and quantification of important chemical constituents in food products and biological samples. The experiments included in this laboratory focus on the estimation and analysis of compounds such as caffeine, amino acids, cholesterol, sugars, urea, machine oil contaminants, oxytocin, capsaicin, and embelin using standard analytical methods.</br>These virtual experiments are designed to help students develop practical knowledge of food analysis, quality assessment, and biochemical testing without the constraints of a physical laboratory. Through simulation-based learning, students can familiarize themselves with laboratory procedures, instrumentation, calculations, data interpretation, and safety considerations. The laboratory aims to strengthen the understanding of food chemistry principles while providing hands-on experience in analytical techniques relevant to food science, biotechnology, chemistry, and related disciplines.

experiments.name: Caffeine content in tea and coffee
Estimation of amino acids in coconut water
Estimation of Cholesterol in edible oils
Estimation of Oxytocin in Various Edibles
Estimation of Embelin content in Embelia ribes (kali mirch) and Tamarind

UG: The Chemistry Virtual Lab is intended for undergraduate (UG) students in science and engineering, as well as faculty members and research scholars.
PG: The Chemistry Virtual Lab is intended for postgraduate (PG) students in science and engineering, as well as faculty members and research scholars.

objective:
The objectives of the Chemistry Virtual Laboratory are to:
  • Familiarize students with analytical methods used for the qualitative and quantitative estimation of chemical constituents in food and biological samples.
  • Develop an understanding of the principles, procedures, and applications of food chemistry and biochemical analysis.
  • Provide practical exposure to laboratory techniques for the estimation of caffeine, amino acids, cholesterol, sugars, urea, capsaicin, embelin, oxytocin, and other analytes.
  • Train students in data collection, calculations, result interpretation, and report preparation.
  • Enhance knowledge of food quality assessment, nutritional analysis, and contaminant detection.
  • Promote safe laboratory practices and scientific problem-solving skills through virtual experimentation.
  • Bridge the gap between theoretical concepts and practical applications in food science, chemistry, biotechnology, and related fields.
